text: ELIZAVILLE, a village of Flemingo co., in the state of Kentucky, U. S., 5 m. W of Flemingsbury. Pop, in 1840, 200.
ELIZONDO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Navarre, partido and 23 m. N of Pamplona, in the valley of Bastan, on the l. bank of the Bidassoa.

ELJAS, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. and 60 m. NNW of Caceres, partido of Los Hoyos, near the foot of the Sierra-de-Gata, and on a branch of a river of the same name. ELJAS, ELJA, ERJAS, or HERJAS, a river which takes its rise in the Sierra-de-Gata, near the E confines of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira; separates that prov. from the Spanish prov. of Caceres; and unites with the Tagus 7 m. SW of Alcantara, after a total course of about 50 m.
ELBEDONI, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 20 m. SW of Abu-Arisch.

ELK, a creek of Pennsylvania, U.S., in Northumberland co., which, uniting with Pern's creek, falls into the Susquehannah 5 m. below Sunbury.- Also a river of Pennsylvania, which rises in Chester co. in two branches,-Big and Little E. creeks,and flows into the N part of Chesapeake bay. It is navigable 15 m. to Elkton.—Also a navigable river of Alabama, U. S., which falls into the Tennessee, a little above the Muscle shoals.—Also a township in Warren co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 382.-Also a township in Athens co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,261.- Also a township in Monroe co., in Ohio. Pop. 535.

ELK-CREEK, a township in Erie co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 16 m. W of Erie. Pop. 1,645.—Also a township in Venango co., in Pennsylvania, 122 m. W by N of Harrisburg. Pop. 785. -Also a v., in Grayson co., in Virginia.
ELK-FORK, a township in Van Buren co., in Missouri. Pop. 360.—Also a township in Pettis co., in Missouri. Pop. 454.
ELK-LAKE, one of the small lakes which connect the Lake-of-the-Woods with Lake Superior, in N lat. 48° 41'.

ELK-RIVER, a river of Virginia, U.S., which runs into the Kanhaway, in N lat. 38° 9'.-Also a river which runs into the Mississippi, in N lat. 43° 42'.—Also a river of North America, which rises in N lat. 53° 40', and falls into the Lake-of-the-Hills, in 58° 36'.—Also a township in Newton co., in Mis-
EL-KAB, a village on the r. bank of the Nile, 40 m. SE of Thebes.
EL-KAIRIE, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 40 m. ENE of Abu-Arisch.
EL-KASR, a village in the oasis of Dakhel, in N lat. 25° 41'.
EL-KASSAREN, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 8 m. E of Shamir.
EL-KATTA, a village of Egypt, 14 m. S of Cairo.

ELKEN, or ITARALTSHIK, a salt lake in the Crimea, to the W of Mount Opuk. It is 27 versts, or about 18 m., in circumf.; and its S point appears to have formerly communicated with the Black sea. It deposits large quantities of salt.

ELKHART, a county in the N of the state of Indiana, U.S. Area 460 sq. m. Pop. 6,660. Its cap. is Goshen.—Also a v. in the same co., 154 m. N of Indianapolis.—Also a v. in Noble co., in Iowa, U. S. Pop. 262.

ELKHORN, a river of Kentucky, U.S., which runs into the Kentucky, on the E side, after a course of 30 m.—Also a fine river of Louisiana, which joins the Platte, about 40 m. before it falls into the Missouri. It is about 80 yds. wide at its mouth, with clear water and a gravelly channel, and is said to be navigable 400 m.
ELKINGTON, a parish in Northamptonshire, 10 m. NNE of Daventry. Area 1,720 acres. Pop. 46.
ELKINGTON (NORTH), a parish in Lincolnshire, 3½ m. NW of Louth. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 91.
ELKINGTON (SOUTH), a parish in Lincolnshire, 2½ m. NW of Louth. Area 2,660 acres. Pop. 273.
ELKLAND, a township in Tioga co., in Pennsylvania, 170 m. N of Harrisburg. Pop. 710.
ELKLICK, a township in Somerset co., in Pennsylvania, 145 m. W of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,495. * 192 ELL

ELKRIDGE, a town of Maryland, on the S bank of Patapsco river, 8 m. W of Baltimore, at the intersection of the Washington with the Baltimore and Ohio railroad.—Also a v. in Giles co., in Tennessee, 65 m. S of Nashville.
ELKSLEY, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 4 m. NW of Taxford. Area 2,500 acres. Pop. 414.
ELSKTONE, a parish in Gloucestershire, 7 m. NNW of Cirencester. Area 2,100 acres. Pop. 335. -Also a township in the p. of Alstonfield, in Staffordshire, 6 m. ENE of Leek. Pop. 772.

ELKTON, a town of Maryland, U.S., at the head of Chesapeake bay, the cap. of Cecil co. It is situated at the forks of Elk river, 15 m. above its mouth. The tide flows up to the town.—Also a v. in Columbiana co., in Ohio, 154 m. NE of Columbus.-Also a v. in Giles co., in Tennessee, 90 m. S of Nashville. Pop. 150.—Also a v. in Todd co., in Kentucky, 187 m. SW of Frankfort.
ELKUVAR, a river of Russia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, which runs into the Tobol, in N lat. 52° 55'.
ELLA-KIRK, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. W of Hull. Area 4,390 acres. Pop. 1,061.
ELLA (WEST), a township in the above p. Area 570 acres. Pop. 102.
ELLAMAITE, a village of Tunis, 30 m. N of Gabes.
ELLAND, a chapelry in the p. of Halifax, W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SE of Halifax, on the Manchester and Leeds railway. Pop. 6,470. Coarse woollens are largely manufactured here.
ELLAR, a small town of the duchy of Nassau, 12 m. SSE of Dillenburg.
ELLASTONE, a parish in Staffordshire, 41 m. WSW of Ashbourne. Area 7,970 acres. Pop. 1,308.
ELLBOGEN. See Elbogen.

ELLDBORG, a volcanic mountain, on the SW coast of Iceland, in Hnappadals syssel, remarkable on account of its singular configuration, and as standing isolated in the middle of an extensive plain which it has deluged with lava. Its oval-shaped crater has a circumf. of 1,800 ft., and is encircled by a wall of dark vitrified lava. Dr. Henderson, who has given a sketch of this mountain in his Journal, says that the name signifies 'the Fortress of fire.'
ELLDEYER, a group of barren and precipitous rocks, the haunt only of sea-fowl, off Cape Reykianess, in the SW point of Iceland.
ELLE', a river of France, which, rising near Gourin, flows S between the deps. of Morbihan and Finistère, and falls into the Atlantic after a course of
ELLEJAY, a village in Gilmer co., in the state of Georgia, U. S., 176 m. NW of Milledgeville, on a stream of the same name, a branch of the Coosa.
ELLEL, a chapelry in the p. of Cockerton, Lancashire, 4 m. SSW of Lancaster. Pop. 2,223.

ELLEN, a small river of Cumberland, taking its rise in Caldbeck-fells, and swelled by numerous streams issuing from the declivities of the range of hills forming the W boundary of the Derwent vale. Having reached the low grounds, it passes Udale and Ireby, and meanders, in a W direction, through an open vale, till it falls into the sea at Maryport.
ELLENBERG, a village of Wurtemberg, 6 m. NE of Ellwangen, with 1,500 inhabitants.
ELLENBOGEN. See Elbogen.
ELLENBOROUGH, a village of Cumberland, in the p. of Durham, 6 m. NW of Cockermouth. Pop. 766. It gives the title of Earl to the Law family.
ELLENBROOK, a hamlet in the p. of Eccles, in Lancashire.
ELLENBURG, a township in Clinton co., in the state of New York, U. S., 25 m. NW of Plattsburg, on English river. Pop. 1,171. . . . . . 1.0 . . * * 3 - . . ELLICHPUR.
SHAH DOOLA. APullarten & Colondon & Edinburgh. RAINAN 0 13 SHEINE 200
ELLENDON, a village in Argyle co., in New South Wales, on the E shore of Lake George.
ELLENHALL, a parish in Staffordshire, 2 m. SE of Eccleshall. Area 1,800 acres. Pop. 280.
ELLENSVILLE, a village in Ulster co., in the state of New York, U. S., 86 m. SW of Albany, on the Delaware and Hudson canal.
ELLERBACH, a river of Rhenish Prussia, which runs into the Moselle, 10 m. above Beilstein.
ELLERBURCH, a small island of Prussia, in the mouth of the Nogat, opposite Elbing.
ELLERBURN, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. ENE of Pickering. Area 2,060 acres. Pop. 216.

ELLERBY, a township in the p. of Lythe, N. R. of Yorkshire, 72 m. NW of Whitby. Area 630 acres. Pop. 78.—Also a township in the p. of Swine, in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. NE of Hull. Area 3,470 acres. Pop. 275.
ELLERO, a stream of the Sardinian states, in the prov. of Corri, falling into the Tanaro on the l. bank.
ELLERSLIE, a village in Susquehanna co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 194 m. NE by N 838. of Harrisburg.—Also a v. in Harris co., in the state of Georgia.
ELLERTON PRIORY, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. NW of Howden. Area 2,320 acres. Pop. 320.

ELLERTON-ON-SWALE, a township in the p. and 2 m. SE of Chatterick, in the N. R. of Yorkshire. Pop. 152. This was the birth-place of Henry Jenkins, who attained to the amazing age of 169. This extraordinary person retained his faculties to the last. His death took place on the 8th December, 1670; he was interred at Bolton-upon-Swale, where a monument was erected to his memory in 1743.
ELLERY, a township in the state of New York, U. S., 343 m. WSW of Albany. Pop. 2,242.
ELLESBOROUGH, a parish in Bucks, 3 m. WSW of Wendover. Area 3,310 acres. Pop. 708.
ELLESHOY, a village of Denmark, in Jutland, S of Aalborg.

ELLESMERE, a town and parish of Salop, 16 m. NNW of Shrewsbury. Area of p., 25,280 acres. Pop. in 1801, 5,909; in 1831, 7,057; in 1841, 7,080. It derives its name from an adjoining lake or mere, 120 acres in extent, bordered on one side by the town, and on the other by Oatley park. The principal traffic is in malt. Commercial intercourse is facilitated by the Ellesmere canal, constructed under an act obtained in 1793. From the Montgomery canal, beginning at Newton, and running by Welshpool, it affords a continuous line of inland navigation to Nantwich, where it communicates with the other Cheshire canals. A railway runs from E. to the collieries of Kynaston.
ELLEZELLES, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 18 m. NE of Tournay. Pop. in 1835, 6,205.
ELLIANT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Rosporden. Pop. 2,789.
ELLICE, a township of Upper Canada, in the Huron district, intersected by a branch of the Thames. Pop. in 1842, 528.

ELLICE ISLAND, an island at the mouth of the Mackenzie river, NW of Langley island. Its NE point is in N lat. 69° 14', W long. 135° 57', and forms the NE entrance to the main channel of the Mackenzie.
ELLICE ISLANDS, a group of coral islets in the Pacific, in S lat. 8° 30', E long. 179° 13'.

ELLICHPUR, a city of India, in the Nizam's territories in Berar, 96 m. W of Nagpur, in N lat. 21° 14', between the Sampun and Bechun rivers, branches of the Purnah. Its fortifications are exten- III ELL sive, but badly planned. There are several fine publie buildings in and around the city.
ELLICOTT, a township in the state of New York, U. S., 18 m. SE of Maryville. Pop. 2,571.
ELLICOTTSVILLE, the cap. of Cattarangus co., in the state of New York, 293 m. W by S of Albany, on Great Valley creek. Pop. 1,084.
ELLIER (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Landivy. Pop. 1,407.
ELLIMARA, or Delimara (Point), a promontory on the SE coast of the island of Malta, the S extremity of a narrow peninsular steep enclosing the E side of the Marsa Scirocco bay.
ELLINGEN, a well-built town of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, 4 m. N of Weissenburg, on the Rezat. Pop. 1,329.

ELLINGHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 23 m. NE of Bungay. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 398.—Also a p. in Northumberland, 8 m. N of Alnwick. Area 13,970 acres. Pop. 861.—Also a p. in Hants, <math>2\frac{1}{2}</math> m. N of Ringwood. Area 1,860 acres. Pop. 301.
ELLINGHAM (GREAT), a parish in Norfolk, 2 m. WNW of Attleborough. Area 2,540 acres. Pop.
ELLINGHAM (LITTLE), a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. NW of Attleborough. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. 250.
ELLINGTON, a parish in Huntingdonshire, 51 m. W of Huntingdon. Area 2,910 acres. Pop. 448.
ELLINGTON, a township in the state of Connecticut, U. S., 20 m. NE of Hartford. Pop. 1,356. -Also a township in the state of New York, 25 m. E of Mayville. Pop. 1,725.

ELLIOT, a stream in the co. of Angus, which falls into the German ocean 2 m. W from Arbroath. -Also a stream in Prince Edward's island, which branches off W from Charlotte-town harbour.—Also a township in the state of Maine, U. S., 102 m. SW of Augusta. Pop. 1,889.
ELLIOT'S ISLAND, a small island in the gulf of Florida, near the coast of E. Florida, in N lat. 25° 32′.
ELLIOT'S (LADY), a low islet on the NE coast of Australia, in S lat. 24° 6', E long. 152° 45' 15". It does not exceed <math>\frac{3}{4}</math> m. in length. [King].

ELLIOT (Mount), a lofty summit in Victorialand, in S lat. 70° 50', E long. 166° 35'. [Ross].— Also a long level hill, with a peak at its N extremity, on the NE coast of Australia, 45 m. W by N of Cape Upstart.
ELLIS ISLAND, a small island at the entrance of the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 24° 50', W long. 81° 10'.—Also an island in the harbour of New York, off the mouth of the Hudson river.
ELLIS (Point), a cape on the W shore of an island on the NW coast of America, in Chatham's strait; the NW point of entrance into a large bay, in N lat. 56° 31'.
ELLISBURG, a township in the state of New York, U. S., 140 m. NW of Albany, on Lake Ontario. Pop. 5,349.
ELLISCHAU, a village of Bohemia, in the circle of Klattau, near which silver mines were once wrought.
ELLISFIELD, a parish in Hants, 4 m. S of Basingstoke. Area 2,360 acres. Pop. 246.
ELLISVILLE, a village in Jones co., in the state of Missouri, U. S., on the W side of Talahala creek.

ELLON, a parish and village in Aberdeenshire. The parish extends about 9 m. in length from N to S, and is about 5 m. in breadth. The v. is pleasantly situated on the Ythan, 17 m. W by N of Aberdeen. Pop. in 1801, 2,022; in 1841, 2,941.

ELLORA, or Elora, a village of Hindostan, in the Nizam's territories, 18 m. NW of Aurungabad, and near the fortress of Dowlatabad, celebrated for its excavated Hindu temples, which in magnitude and perfection of execution surpass any thing of the kind elsewhere known. The whole excavations bear the title of Kailasa, or 'Paradise;' and are not less than 14 m. in length. There are 16 caves, wrought out of the solid granite, many of them of two stories, and some of three; and most of them are not less than from 100 to 150 ft. in depth. "The first view of this desolate religious city," says Mr. Erskine, "is grand and striking, but melanchely. The number and magnificence of the subterraneous temples, the extent and loftiness of some, the endless diversity of sculpture in others, the variety of curious foliage, of minute tracery, highly wrought pillars, rich mythological designs, sacred shrines, and colossal statues, astonish but distract the mind. From their number and diversity, it is impossible to form any idea of the whole; and the first impressions only give way to a wonder not less natural, that such prodigious efforts of labour and skill should remain, from times certainly not barbarous, without a trace to tell us the hand by which they were designed, or the populous and powerful nation by which they were completed. The empire, whose pride they must have been, has passed away, and left not a memorial behind it. The religion to which we owe one part of them, indeed, continues to exist; but that which called into existence the other, like the beings by whose toil it was wrought, has been swept from the land." The excavations are divided by Mr. Erskine into three classes: the northern, which are Budhist, or rather Jain,—the central, which are Brahminical,—and the southern, which are certainly Budhist.

ELLORE, one of the five districts called the Northern circars, situated on the W side of the bay of Bengal, now comprised in the collectorate of Masulipatam. It is estimated to contain 2,700 sq. m. of arable or pasture land. It is well-watered, and productive, particularly of sugar cane, and is said to have formerly possessed a diamond mine.—The cap., of the same name, is situated 38 m. N of Masulipatam, on the N side of the Colair lake, through which it has been proposed to carry a canal from the Godavery to the Kistnah. It possesses a small fort, and formerly had a military cantonment in its vicinity.
ELLOUGH, a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. SE of Beccles. Area 500 acres. Pop. 155.

ELLOUGHTON, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SE of S. Cave. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 664, of whom 170 are in the hamlet of Brough, which has a station on the Hull and Selby railway <math>10\frac{1}{4}</math> m. from Hull and <math>20\frac{3}{4}</math> m. from Selby, and is a growing place.
ELLRICH, a town of Prussian Saxony, in the regency and 45 m. NW of Erfurt, on the Zorge. Pop. 2,600.

ELLSWORTH, a township in the state of Maine, U. S., 89 m. ENE of Augusta, on Union river. Pop. 2,263.—Also a township in Grafton co., in New Hampshire, 52 m. N by W of Concord. Pop. 300. —Also a township in Trumbull co., in Ohio, 160 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 988.

ELLWANGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, formerly the cap. of a district of the same name, and now the chief town in the circle of the Jaxt, situated in a pleasant valley on the Jaxt, 45 m. ENE of Stuttgard. Pop. in 1840, 3,799. It is small but well built; and has a castle, cathedral, and gymnasium.
ELM, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 8 m. SSE of Glaris, near the frontiers of the Grisons, and closely surrounded by mountains. Pop. 1,013.
ELM, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 2 m. S of Wisbeach. Area 11,230 acres. Pop. 1,742.—Also a p. in Somersetshire, 2 m. WNW of Frome. Area 820 acres. Pop. 421.
ELMA-DAGH, a mountain-range in Asiatic ELM Turkey, to the S of Angora, on the skirts of Caramania.
EL-MAHAD, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 8 m. NNW of Zebid.
ELMANCHILLY, a town of Hindostan, in the circar of Cicacole, 8 m. SW of Cossimcotta.
ELMBRIDGE, a chapelry in the p. of Dodderhill, in Worcestershire, 4 m. N of Droitwich. Pop. 384.
ELMDON, a parish in Warwickshire, 5 m. SW of Coleshill. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 167.—Also a p. in Essex, 6 m. W by N of Saffron-Walden. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 680.
ELME (SAINT), a small fortress of France, in the Pyrenees, in the cant. of Argèles, near Collioure and the Spanish frontier.

ELMHAM (North), a village and parish of Norfolk, 5½ m. N of E. Dereham. Area of p. 5,000 acres. Pop. 1,219. It was once a bishop's see, said to have been founded about the year 673; united in 870 with Dunwich, and removed to Thetford. Many Roman antiquities have been found in the neighbourhood.
ELMHULT, a small town of Sweden, in the prov. of Wexiö, near the Möceln lake.

ELMINA, or St. George del Mina, the cap. of the Dutch settlements in W. Africa, and the most respectable fortress on the Gold coast, in N lat. 5° 4' 45", W long. 1° 20' 30", 9 m. to the W of Cape Coast settlement. It was built in 1481, by the Portuguese, who made it the cap. of their settlements upon this coast, of which they then claimed the sovereignty. In 1637 it was taken by the Dutch; and it was ceded to that people by treaty in 1641. The Dutch from that time claimed the sole dominion of the Gold coast, but were soon forced by the English to recede from that pretension. E. is seated on a peninsula formed by a small river which runs nearly parallel with the sea. The main fortress is extensive, its works mounting 90 guns. It has two approaches; one from the town, which is strengthened by a double ditch over which are 2 drawbridges; the other adjoining the river, which has a small gate, at an elevation of about 12 ft., to which an ascent is formed by means of a steep ladder. It is also strengthened by a fort built on an eminence, about musketshot from the castle, named Conradsburg or St. Jago, which may be considered the key of E. The town is large, and remarkably dirty; some of the houses are built of stone, but they are huddled together in a confused manner, and the streets are narrow, crooked, and dirty. One section of the town, however, has long and wide streets, two of which, meeting at an obtuse angle, form an alameda of nearly a mile in extent, and shaded by a double row of trees. The river, though small, will admit vessels of 100 tons burden at high-water, and they may load and unload under the walls of the castle. This is an important advantage for throwing supplies into the latter; yet its strength by good judges is supposed to be by no means great. The walls are low, and so situated that cannon cannot be used with advantage, nor can musketry be employed without exposing the garrison. Notwithstanding these defects, it repulsed a respectable English force in 1781; but this is said to have been owing to a want of concert between the assailing commanders. The most sickly months here are July, August, and September. The rainy months are May, June, and July. March and April are the hottest months; August and September the coldest. The native name of this place is Addina.

ELMIRA, a township of Chemung co., in the state of New York, U. S., 195 m. WSW of Albany, bounded on the SW by Newton's creek, intersected by the New York and Erie railroad, and at the terminus of the Chemung canal. It presents an irregular surface, but in the more level tracts and valleys is very fertile. Pop. in 1840, 4,791. The village, formerly called Newtown, is situated at the confluence of Newtown creek with the Chemung river.
ELMIRE WITH CRAKEHILL, a township in the p. of Topcliffe, N. R. of Yorkshire. Area 900 acres. Pop. 93.
ELMLEY-CASTLE, a parish of Worcestershire, 4 m. WSW of Evesham, and SE of the Avon. Area terest. 2,260 acres. Pop. 403.
ELMLEY (ISLE OF), a parish of Kent, in the isle of Sheppey, 31 m. ESE of Queenborough, 3 m. in length, and 2 m. in breadth. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 42.
ELMLEY-LOVETT, a parish of Worcestershire, 5 m. SSE of Kidderminster. Area 2,350 acres. Pop. 381.
ELMO (SAINT), an island of the South Pacific, in the Low archipelago, in S lat. 21° 30', W long. 143° 40'. It was discovered by Quiros in 1606.
ELMO, or ELIMO (SAN). See VALETTA.
ELMORE, a parish of Gloucestershire, 5 m. WSW of Gloucester. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 379.

ELMORE, a township of Lamoille co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 20 m. N of Montpelier. It has a hilly surface, and is watered by affluents of Lamoille and Onion rivers. Its soil possesses considerable fertility. Pop. in 1840, 476.

ELMSALL (NORTH and SOUTH), townships in the p. of South Kirby, W. R. of Yorkshire. The former is 6\fract m. SE of Pontefract. Area 1,740 acres. Pop. 281. The latter is 7 m. SE of Pontefract. Area 1,740 acres. Pop. 518.
ELMSETT, a parish of Suffolk, 4 m. NNE of Hadleigh. Area 1,810 acres. Pop. 446.

ELMSHORN, a town of Denmark, in the duchy of Holstein, on the river Aue, 18 m. NW of Hamburg. Pop. 5,200, of whom a considerable number are Jews. It is a thriving place, and specially noted for its large manufacture of shoes, employing nearly 600 hands. The greater part of the town was consumed by fire in 1750, but afterwards rebuilt on an improved plan.

ELMSLEY (North), a township of Upper Canada, in the Bathurst district, bounded on the SE by Rideau lake and canal. Pop. in 1842, 1,154. It is generally fertile, and has good timber. In the E of the t., on Rideau canal, is the flourishing village of Smith's Falls, and on Rideau lake is Oliver's Ferry.

ELMSLEY (South), a township of Upper Canada, in Johnson district, bounded on the N by Rideau canal and Otter lake, and intersected by the Tay, an affluent of Rideau lake. The soil is to a considerable extent of good quality, and covered with good timber. Pop. 815, chiefly Irish and Scotch.
ELMSTEAD, a parish of Essex, 4 m. NE of Colchester. Area 3,490 acres. Pop. 809.—Also a parish of Kent, 7 m. NE of Ashford. Area 2,530 acres. Pop. 505.
ELMSTHORPE, a parish of Leicestershire, 3 m. ENE of Hinckley. Area 1,650 acres. Pop. 36.
ELMSTONE, a parish of Kent, 24 m. NNE of Wingham, and S of the Stour. Area 360 acres. Pop. 81.
ELMSTONE-HARDWICKE, a parish of Gloucestershire, 31 m. NNW of Cheltenham, near the Birmingham and Gloucester railway. Area 2,730 acres. Pop. 410.
ELMSWELL, a parish of Suffolk, 6 m. NW of Stow-market. Area 2,380 acres. Pop. 671.
ELMTON, a parish of Derbyshire, 8 m. NE of Chesterfield. Area 2,970 acres. Pop. 433.

ELNE, a commune and town of France, in the Catholics. dep. of the Pyrénées-Orientales, cant. and 9 m. SE of Perpignan, on the l. bank of the Tech. Pop. 2,303. This town is of great antiquity, and was for- - ELP merly an episcopal see. It was originally named Illiberir, and afterwards Helena, after the mother of Constantine, by whom it was rebuilt and fortified. It was occupied by Hannibal on his first entrance into France, and sustained sieges in 1289, 1474, and 1641. With the exception of its ancient cathedral, it now possesses no objects of architectural in-

ELOISE, a village of Sardinia, in Savoy, prov. of Carouge, 15 m. WSW of Saint-Julien, on an acclivity on the l. bank of the Rhone, 2 m. SE of the place named Perte du Rhone, where that river forces its passage under ground.
ELORA. See ELLORA.

ELORRIO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Viscaya or Biscay, partido and 6 m. SE of Durango, near the l. bank of the Orrio. Pop. 2,280. It has 2 parish-churches, several convents, and an hospital. Ironmongery is manufactured here, and a fair is held once a-year.
ELORY, or CELHUY, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Pyrénées, cant. and com. of Hasparrin. Pop. 1,104.

ELOUGES, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 12 m. W of Mons. Pop. 2,296. It has extensive sugar and salt-refineries, rope-works, manufactories of tobacco, and lime-kilns. Coal is also wrought in the locality.
ELOY (SAINT), a commune and coal-basin of France, in the dep. of the Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Montaigut. Pop. 798. The annual produce of the coal-mines of the locality is calculated at 40,000 q.
ELOY-DE-GY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Cher, cant. of Saint-Martin-d'Auxigny. Pop. 1,008.
ELOYES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Remirement. Pop. 984.
ELPARE (MONTE), a town of the States-of-the- Church, in the deleg. and 14 m. SW of Fermo, near the l. bank of the Aso. Pop. 1,000.

ELPHIN, a parish, containing the episcopal town of the same name, in co. Roscommon. Area 12,544 acres, of which 426 acres are water. Pop. in 1831, 6,643; in 1841, 6,781. The land is to a large extent disposed in some of the richest grazing-ground in the kingdom.—The post, market, and episcopal town of E. is situated 5½ m. NNW of Strokestown, and 77 m. WNW of Dublin. It extends along the summit of a ridge nearly E and W; and consists chiefly of one winding and spacious street. The whole town, with the exception of its two or three public buildings, consists chiefly of cabins, the greater number wretched huts. The cathedral measures about 80 ft. by 28; and, except for having 4 round-topped windows in the side next the street, looks not unlike an English barn jostled up against an ancient steeple; it has, however, a neat interior. The palace, situated on the road to Boyle, a 1/4 m. from the church, is a comfortable old-fashioned country house. Pop. of the town in 1831, 1,507; in 1841, 1,551. Previous to the act of 1833, the bishopric existed without any modern annexation; but that act provided for its permanent union with the sees of Kilmore and Ardagh. -The diocese is 30 m. in length, and from 3 to 30 m. in breadth. Area 489,333 acres. Pop. in 1831, 317,197. It includes by far the greater part of co. Roscommon, a large part of co. Sligo, a considerable part of co. Galway, and a very small portion of co. Mayo. In 1834, the pop. consisted of 16,417 members of the Establishment, 250 Presbyterians, 135 other Protestant dissenters, and 310,822 Roman
ELPHINSTONE, a village in the p. of Tranent, East Lothian. Pop. 236.
ELPHINSTON'S BAY, an indentation of the pelago, in N lat. 0° 30'.
ELPIDIO-MORICO (SANTO), a town of the Pontifical states, in the delegation and 5 m. N of Fermo. Pop. 291. Grain and wine are extensively cultivated in the environs.
ELRINGTON (CAPE), a headland of Russian America, at the S extremity of a craggy island, to the SW of Prince William's sound, in N lat. 59° 54'.

ELS, a village of Lower Austria, in the circle and 10½ m. WNW of Krems. Pop. 206.—Also a v. of Moravia, 32 m. W of Olmutz.—Also a river of the grand-duchy of Oldenburg, which runs into the Weser 20 m. below Bremen.

ELSA, a river of Tuscany, rising in the Colle-di- Manciano, and flowing into the Albagna near Marsigliana, after a course of 12 m.—Also a small river of Upper Silesia, which, after a course to the W and N, falls into the Oder at Oderberg.
ELSASS, a river of Baden, which rises near Eppingen, and runs into the Neckar at Neckar-Gemund.
ELSASS. See ALSACE.
ELSBACH (OBER), a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Franconia, 5 m. from Fladungen. Pop. 962.
ELSDON, a market-town and parish of Northumberland, 16 m. WNW of Morpeth. Area of p. 62,470 acres. Pop. 1,680.
ELSE, a village of Prussia, at the conflux of the Alme and Lippe, 2 m. WNW of Paderborn. — Also a river of Prussia, which runs into the Drausen lake 5 m. W of Marienfeldt.
ELSEN, a village of Prussia, in the circle of the Rhine, 9 m. NE of Juliers. Pop. 620.
ELSENS. See Alsens.
